headline: Northern Pacific Railroad Reaches Missoula, 1883
body: Well now, if you ever heard tell of a town just itching for a bit of progress, Missoula, Montana Territory, in the year of our Lord 1883, was just such a place. Tucked away in them grand valleys, it was a fine spot, no doubt, but getting anything in or out was a trial fit for a saint or a mule, and sometimes you couldn't tell the difference. Folks relied on riverboats when the water was high enough, and wagons when it wasn't, and the pack-trains, bless their dusty souls, only ran when the weather saw fit. It was a slow dance with isolation, and the good people of Missoula, they were mighty tired of leading. They yearned for a connection, a proper link to the wider world, and that link, they knew, had iron wheels and a plume of smoke. The Northern Pacific Railroad, that grand serpent of steel, was slithering its way across the continent, and every soul in Missoula watched its progress like a hawk watches a field mouse. The newspapers, they weren't much for gossip in those days, but they sure did keep a running tally of every foot of track laid. By early June of '83, the talk wasn't just talk anymore; it was the rumble of an approaching engine. The Weekly Missoulian, a paper with its ear to the ground and its eye on the horizon, reported with no small measure of glee, "On Tuesday, June 5, 12,200 feet of track were laid. Mr. Winston has promised all his men a new hat if they get to Missoula by the 26th of June." Now, a new hat might not sound like much to a city slicker, but out on that frontier, it was a promise worth sweating for. The editor himself, a man of some foresight and a good deal of curiosity, took a carriage ride out west of town a couple of weeks later. He saw the iron horse, a sight that, as he put it, sent "a thrill of pleasure ran over our frame as we thought how many years the coming of that locomotive had been looked for by the old-timers of Missoula." And sure enough, those track layers, spurred on by the promise of headwear and the sheer will of progress, made good time. On Saturday, June 23, 1883, at precisely 4:50 in the afternoon, the first train, Engine No. 452, chugged its way into Missoula. The town erupted. It wasn't just a train; it was a declaration. The New Northwest's Missoula correspondent, a man clearly not afraid of a bit of flourish, declared, "... the long agony is over and Missoula is a railroad town." He went on to paint a picture of a celebration that would make a lesser town blush: "Engine No. 452 steamed slowly inside the city limits, and was welcomed by the thunder of cannon, a lavish display of bunting, the cheers of the assembled populace, and last but not least the opening of Frank Worden's celebrated barrel of N.P. whiskey, which has been hid in Worden & Co.'s cellar for about twenty years." Now, that whiskey, that's a story in itself. Back in '69, a fellow named Sam Wilkinson from the Northern Pacific had visited Missoula, talking railroad prospects. Over a drink or two at Worden & Company, he, Higgins, and Worden had set aside a barrel, a promise in oak and spirits, to be opened when the railroad finally arrived. Wilkinson, too old and frail to travel by '83, sent a letter, instructing them to share the "thirsting crowd," but to save a quarter of the barrel, his "precious and patriotic residue," for him. The railroad didn't just bring whiskey and cheers; it brought change, swift and irreversible. Missoula, once a mere trading post and lumber camp, was now a proper hub, a gateway. Goods flowed in and out with a speed that would have seemed miraculous just weeks before. Timber, minerals, farm produce, all found their way to market, and manufactured goods, along with a steady stream of new faces, poured into Montana. The isolation was broken, the frontier pushed back a notch, and Missoula, with its new iron lifeline, was ready to embrace its destiny as a bustling center of commerce and industry in the wild, wide West.
